no_solution_leakage.reason: The instruction explicitly states the exact implementation approach: 'Buffer prototype provides an efficient method for extracting latin1-encoded substrings', 'Pre-allocated reusable arrays would eliminate allocation overhead', 'threshold should be increased substantially' (tests check for exactly 64), 'batch the character code generation using pre-allocated arrays', and 'sourceTextLatin' variable naming is effectively leaked through the detailed description of what to implement.

solution_uniqueness_guard.reason: Tests assert exact variable names like 'sourceTextLatin', 'stringDecodeArrays', 'sourceEndPos', exact array size 'Array(65).fill(null)', exact threshold 'if (len > 64)', exact method call 'fromCharCode.apply(null, arr)', exact destructuring '{ latin1Slice } = Buffer.prototype'. An alternative correct fix using different variable names (e.g., 'latinSourceText'), different array sizes (e.g., 128), or different thresholds (e.g., 48 or 128) would fail despite being functionally correct.

tests_verify_behavior_not_text.reason: The fail-to-pass tests (test_latin1slice_usage, test_source_text_latin_variable, test_string_decode_arrays, test_64_byte_threshold, test_from_char_code_apply, test_source_end_pos, test_raw_transfer_generator_updated) are ALL text-matching assertions using 'assert "..." in content'. Only the pass-to-pass tests (test_napi_parser_tests, test_napi_parser_esm, test_napi_parser_visit) execute actual code.

test_not_tautological.reason: The f2p tests only check for string presence in files. An agent could simply insert comments or dead code containing the required strings (e.g., '// stringDecodeArrays = Array(65).fill(null)') into the generated files without implementing any actual optimization, and all f2p tests would pass.
